question: Which type of Java statement allows you to execute a block of code repeatedly?
description: Iteration statements (while, do-while, for) repeat a block of code.

question: What is the correct syntax for a while loop in Java?
description: The condition goes in parentheses, the body in braces: while (expr) { ... }.

question: In a while loop, when is the condition checked?
option_d: At the end of the loop
description: A while loop tests its condition at the top, before each iteration — so it may run zero times.

question: What are the four essentials of a loop statement?
option_b: Control variable, initial value, increment/decrement, continuation condition
description: Every controlled loop needs a control variable, an initial value, an update, and a continuation condition.

question: What happens if the while loop condition never becomes false?
description: If the condition stays true forever, you get an infinite loop.

question: Given: int count = 0; while (count < 5) { count++; } — how many times does the loop execute?
description: count takes 0,1,2,3,4 (5 iterations); when it reaches 5 the condition is false.

question: What is a 'sentinel value' in the context of a loop?
option_a: A value used to initialize the loop counter
option_b: A special input value that signals the end of input
description: A sentinel is a special value (e.g. 0 or -1) that tells the loop to stop reading input.

question: Why should floating-point values NOT be used for equality checking in a loop condition?
option_b: Floating-point values are approximations and may never be exactly equal
option_c: Java does not support floating-point comparisons
option_d: They cause compile errors in loops
description: Floating-point rounding means a value may never be exactly equal to the target, risking an infinite loop. Use </> instead.

question: What does count++ do inside a while loop?
description: count++ adds 1 to count (post-increment).

question: What does the break statement do inside a loop?
option_a: Skips the current iteration and moves to the next
option_c: Restarts the loop from the beginning
description: break exits the loop entirely, jumping to the statement after it.

question: After a break statement executes inside a while loop, where does control go?
option_b: To the beginning of the loop body
option_c: To the first statement after the loop
description: Control transfers to the first statement after the loop.

question: What does the continue statement do inside a loop?
option_b: Jumps to the next iteration, skipping the remaining statements in the current iteration
description: continue skips the rest of the current iteration and re-evaluates the loop condition.

question:
What is the output?
for(int i=1;i<=5;i++){ if(i==3) continue; System.out.print(i); }
description: When i==3, continue skips the print, so output is 1 2 4 5.

question:
What is the value of i after this loop?
int i=0; while(true){ if(i==3) break; i++; }
description: i increments to 3, then the break fires while i equals 3.

question: Which statement is used to print only ODD numbers from 1–10 using a loop?
description: if(i%2==0) continue; skips even numbers, leaving only the odd ones to print.

question: What is the key difference between break and continue?
option_a: break skips one iteration; continue ends the loop
option_b: break ends the loop entirely; continue skips to the next iteration
description: break = full loop exit; continue = skip the current iteration only.

question: What is the main difference between a while loop and a do-while loop?
option_a: do-while checks the condition before execution
option_b: do-while always executes the body at least once
option_c: while loop executes at least once
description: A do-while checks the condition at the bottom, so the body always runs at least once.

question: Where does the do-while loop evaluate its condition?
option_a: At the top of the loop
option_c: At the bottom of the loop
description: do { ... } while(cond); tests the condition at the bottom.

question: What is the correct syntax for a do-while loop in Java?
description: The trailing while (condition); — including the semicolon — is mandatory.

question: If the condition in a do-while loop is false from the start, how many times does the body execute?
description: Because the check is at the bottom, the body runs once before the condition is ever tested.

question: A do-while loop is best suited for which scenario?
option_a: When you need to repeat a fixed number of times
option_b: When the loop must execute at least once before checking a condition
option_d: When the number of iterations is unknown and may be zero
description: Input validation (prompt, then check) is the classic do-while use case.

question: int x = 10; do { x--; } while (x > 15); — What is x after the loop?
description: The body runs once (x → 9), then 9 > 15 is false, ending the loop.

question: A program prompts the user to enter a positive number and repeats if negative. Which loop is most appropriate?
description: The prompt must appear at least once, so a do-while is ideal.

question: Which statement about do-while is TRUE?
option_a: It checks the condition before executing the body
option_b: It can never create an infinite loop
option_c: The loop body is guaranteed to execute at least once
option_d: It does not support break statements
description: Guaranteed single execution is the defining property of do-while.

question: What is the correct syntax of a for loop in Java?
description: The three parts of a for header are separated by semicolons.

question: In a for loop, when does the initialization expression execute?
option_b: Only when the condition is true
option_c: Once, at the beginning of the loop
description: Initialization runs once at the start of the loop.

question: What is the output of: for(int i=0; i<3; i++) { System.out.print(i+" "); }
description: i starts at 0 and stops when i==3, printing 0 1 2.

question: Which parts of a for loop header are optional (can be omitted)?
description: All three are optional; for(;;) is an infinite loop equivalent to while(true).

question: How many times does for(int i=1; i<=10; i+=2) execute?
description: i takes 1, 3, 5, 7, 9 → 5 iterations.

question: What does the termination expression in a for loop determine?
option_b: Whether the loop continues or stops
description: When the termination (condition) expression becomes false, the loop ends.

question: Which for loop correctly prints numbers 1 through 10?
description: Starting at 1 with i<=10 prints exactly 1–10.

question: What is a nested loop?
option_b: A loop that contains one or more loops inside it
description: A nested loop has an inner loop placed inside an outer loop.

question: With an outer loop running 3 times and an inner loop running 4 times, how many total inner-loop iterations occur?
description: The inner loop runs fully on each outer pass: 3 × 4 = 12.

question: In a 12×12 multiplication table built with nested loops, what does the outer loop typically control?
description: The outer loop iterates rows; the inner loop iterates columns within each row.

question: What is a method in Java?
option_b: A collection of statements grouped to perform an operation
description: A method is a named block of statements that performs a specific task.

question: Which is NOT a benefit of using methods?
description: Methods aid reuse, reduce complexity, and hide details — they do not permanently increase memory.

question: In the method header public int max(int num1, int num2), what is 'int' before 'max'?
description: The type before the method name is the return type — what the method gives back.

question: What keyword is used when a method does not return any value?
description: void means the method performs actions but returns nothing.

question: What access modifier makes a method visible everywhere (to the whole world)?
description: public gives the widest visibility — accessible from any class.

question: Variables defined in a method header are called:
description: Parameters declared in the method definition are formal parameters.

question: Values passed to a method when calling it are called:
description: The values supplied at the call site are actual parameters (arguments).

question: What is 'variable scope' in Java?
option_b: The region of code where a variable is accessible
option_c: The data type of a variable
option_d: How many times a variable is used
description: Scope is the region of code in which a variable can be referenced.

question: A variable declared inside a for loop { } block has which scope?
description: Variables declared inside braces have block scope — usable only within that block.

question: Which access modifier makes a method visible only within its own class?
description: private restricts visibility to the declaring class only.

question: Given public int add(int a, int b) { return a+b; }, what is the correct way to call it?
description: Because it returns an int, store the result: int result = add(3, 4);.

question: Does a while loop always execute its body at least once?
option_b: No — if the condition is false initially, the body never runs
option_d: Only when the counter starts at 0
description: A while checks the condition first, so the body may run zero times. (A do-while runs at least once.)

question: Are all three parts of a for loop header (init, condition, update) mandatory?
option_b: No — all three are optional; for(;;) is valid
description: All three parts are optional; for(;;) creates an infinite loop.

question: Is a variable declared inside a method accessible outside that method?
option_b: No — method-scoped variables exist only within the method
option_c: Yes, if it is an int
option_d: Only if it is declared public
description: Local (method-scoped) variables are not visible outside their method.

question: Are public methods visible only within the class they are defined in?
option_a: Yes — public restricts to the class
option_b: No — public methods are visible everywhere
description: public gives world visibility; private restricts to the class.

question: Does a nested loop's inner loop restart from scratch each time the outer loop iterates?
option_a: Yes — it re-initializes and re-runs fully on every outer iteration
option_b: No — it continues from where it stopped
option_c: Only if it uses a different counter
description: Each outer pass causes the inner loop to re-initialize and run completely again.

question: Does the protected access modifier make a method visible to the package and all subclasses?
option_d: Only the subclasses, never the package
description: protected visibility covers the declaring package and all subclasses.
